title: Tighten deterministic chat parser regexes to prevent false positive matches
description:
## Problem
The deterministic `ChatInterpreter.ParseIntent` regexes are too broad, causing false positive matches when keywords appear incidentally in longer messages.

### False positive #1: "enough" → cancel
Message: "What do you have access to? Is this enough to play minecraft?"
Pattern: `\b(stop|cancel|enough|quit|abort|nevermind|never mind)\b`
The word "enough" in "Is this enough" triggers an emergency stop.

### False positive #2: "help" → help response
Message: "What other chat harness -> msa agent utilities would help?"
Pattern: `\b(help|commands|what can you do|usage)\b`
The word "help" appearing at the end of a longer question triggers the canned help response instead of reaching the LLM.

## Fix
1. **Cancel pattern**: Remove "enough" — it's too common in everyday language to be a reliable cancel signal. Keep `stop|cancel|quit|abort|nevermind|never mind` which are unambiguous.
2. **Help pattern**: Add a message length gate so "help"/"commands"/"usage" only match when the message is short (≤ 40 chars), preventing incidental word matches in longer sentences. Multi-word patterns like "what can you do" are less prone to false positives and remain length-unrestricted.

## Files
- `Agent.Planning/ChatInterpreter.cs` — cancel regex + help regex + message length check

## Evidence
From session log 2026-06-27:
```
[10:02:37] [chat] <TheMasonX23> What do you have access to? Is this enough to play minecraft? → cancel (emergency stop)
[10:13:18] [chat] <TheMasonX23> What other chat harness -> msa agent utilities would help? → help (canned response)
```

## Priority
High — these false positives disrupt user conversation and bypass the LLM.
